title: Baby massage and which oils
excerpt: In Ayurveda, the daily oil massage of a baby is not a luxury, it is care: a way to build strength, settle sleep, and steady a newborn's growing body. Here is the classical view, and how to do it gently at home.
body_html:
the everyday ritual, and what Ayurveda calls it

Almost every Indian household knows the scene: a warm afternoon, a grandmother or a maalishwali seated on the floor, a baby laid across her outstretched legs, and slow strokes of oil from shoulders to toes. The baby fusses for a minute, then settles, and often sleeps deeply afterwards. This is not just tradition. Ayurveda treats it as a daily practice with a name and a purpose.

The Sanskrit word is abhyanga, which simply means oil massage applied to the body. For babies and children, the classical pediatric text Kashyapa Samhita gives it real importance, describing oiling, bathing, and care of the infant as part of how a child is raised to be strong and well (Kashyapa Samhita, Sutrasthana). The general principle that regular abhyanga nourishes the body, builds firmness, and is especially suited to those who are still growing is stated in the broader texts too (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2; Charaka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 5).

why oil, warmth and gentle hands matter

A newborn's body, in Ayurvedic terms, is soft and still forming, and the air-and-movement quality called Vata is naturally high in early life. Vata is dry, light and cold by nature, and oil is its direct opposite: unctuous, warming and grounding. This is the simple logic behind daily massage. Regular abhyanga is described as relieving dryness, soothing Vata, strengthening the body, and improving sleep and skin (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2). For a baby who cannot yet tell you what unsettles it, steady warm strokes are a way of calming the nervous, restless quality from the outside in.

Warmth is half the practice. The oil should be gently warm, never hot, and the room should be free of draughts, because cold undoes the whole point. The texts pair massage with a warm bath afterwards, and warn against bathing or oiling against the season or in a chilled state. The hands matter as much as the oil: slow, soft, confident strokes, not deep pressure. A baby's bones and joints are tender, so this is reassurance, not physiotherapy.

which oils the texts favour

The single most recommended base oil in the classical texts is sesame oil, called tila taila. Sushruta calls sesame oil the best of the oils for massage because it is warming, penetrating and strengthening (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 45). For most babies in most seasons, plain, good-quality sesame oil, gently warmed, is the safe classical default.

Ayurveda also adjusts the oil to the weather and the child. In hot weather or for a baby that runs warm, a cooler choice such as coconut oil suits better, since coconut is light and cooling. Many families also use medicated oils like Bala taila, prepared with the herb Bala, which the tradition associates with building strength in children. The deeper teaching is matching the oil to the season and the baby's nature rather than fixing on one bottle: cooling oils in heat, warming oils in cold (Charaka Samhita, Sutrasthana; Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Mustard oil is common in many homes and can be warming, but it is sharp, so if you use it, keep it well diluted, gently warmed, and watch the skin.

a gentle way to do it at home

Pick a warm, quiet time when the baby is calm and not just fed, ideally a little before the bath. Warm a small amount of oil to body temperature by standing the bowl in warm water, and always test a drop on your own wrist first. Start at the head with the lightest touch, then work over the chest and tummy in soft circles, down the arms and legs with long strokes, and finally the back. The whole thing need not take long; ten to fifteen unhurried minutes is plenty. Let the oil stay a short while, then give a warm, gentle bath and keep the baby cosy and out of cold air afterwards.

Do a small patch test with any new oil before a full massage, and stop if the skin reddens or the baby seems distressed. Never massage over an unhealed navel stump, a rash, a wound, or a feverish baby, and avoid getting oil in the eyes or ears.

when to see a doctor

Baby massage is gentle daily care, not treatment. See a paediatrician or a qualified vaidya, and do not rely on massage, if your baby has a fever, is feeding poorly or losing weight, has a spreading rash, broken or weeping skin, an infected or oozing navel, swelling or pain when a limb is moved, breathing trouble, or persistent crying that does not settle. Premature or unwell newborns and any underlying condition need a doctor's guidance before you begin a daily routine. This article is general information drawn from classical texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care for your child.

Try This Today

  • Warm the oil the safe way: Stand the oil bowl in a larger bowl of warm water rather than heating it directly. Test a drop on the inside of your wrist; it should feel pleasantly warm, never hot.
  • Start light, start at the head: Begin with the lightest touch on the head, then move to chest, tummy, arms, legs and back. Soft circles on the torso, long slow strokes down the limbs. No pressure on joints.
  • Pick the moment: Massage when the baby is calm and not just fed, a little before the bath. Ten to fifteen unhurried minutes is enough, followed by a warm bath and cosy clothes.
  • Patch-test new oils: Before a full massage with any new or medicated oil, rub a little on a small patch of skin and wait. If it reddens or the baby is distressed, stop and choose a milder oil.
  • Adjust to the weather: In cold months lean on warming sesame oil; in summer heat or for a warm-natured baby, a lighter, cooling oil such as coconut suits better.

metafields.dek: In Ayurveda, the daily oil massage of a baby is not a luxury, it is care: a way to build strength, settle sleep, and steady a newborn's growing body. Here is the classical view, and how to do it gently at home.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Daily oil massage (abhyanga) is classical baby care: it soothes the restless Vata quality, builds strength and helps sleep.
Sesame oil (tila taila) is the texts' favourite base; switch to a cooling oil like coconut in hot weather, and match the oil to the season.

Keep the oil gently warm, hands soft, the room draught-free, and follow with a warm bath; never massage a feverish or unwell baby or an unhealed navel, and see a paediatrician or qualified vaidya if anything seems wrong.

glossary_terms.definition: Oil massage applied to the body; for babies, a daily nourishing and calming practice.
Sesame oil, the classical first-choice oil for massage: warming, penetrating and strengthening.
The air-and-movement quality in the body, dry, light and cold by nature, and naturally high in newborns.
A herb (and a medicated oil made with it) the tradition links with building strength, often used for children.

sources: Kashyapa Samhita, infant care and massage (Kaumarabhritya)
Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 45 (sesame oil as best for massage)
Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana, Ch. 2 (Dinacharya, benefits of abhyanga)
Charaka Samhita, Sutrasthana, Ch. 5 (Matrashitiya, daily abhyanga)

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "Abhyanga" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, Abhyanga is defined as: Oil massage applied to the body; for babies, a daily nourishing and calming practice.

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to warm the oil the safe way. Stand the oil bowl in a larger bowl of warm water rather than heating it directly. Test a drop on the inside of your wrist; it should feel pleasantly warm, never hot.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to start light, start at the head. Begin with the lightest touch on the head, then move to chest, tummy, arms, legs and back. Soft circles on the torso, long slow strokes down the limbs. No pressure on joints.
